coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical synthesis of a multi-responsive regenerable water-stable zinc(II) fluorescent probe for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of benzaldehyde, ferric ion and PH
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin Rui
Wang, Xing Ze
Li, Yong
Liu, Kun
Liu, Shi Xin
Du, Jing
Huang, Zhuo
Luo, Yan
Huo, Jian Zhong
Wu, Xiang Xia
Liu, Yuan Yuan
Ding, Bin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel water-stable coordination polymer with {44} network topology {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1) (L = 4,4′-Bis(triazol-1-ylmethyl)biphenyl) has been synthesized through the hydrothermal and sonochemical approaches. 1 has been characterized by single crystal X-ray diffraction, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectrum and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). PXRD patterns of the as-synthesized samples 1 have confirmed the purity of the bulky samples. In the sonochemical preparation approaches, different ultrasound irradiation power and ultrasound time were also used in order to investigate the impact factor for morphology and size of nano-structured 1. Photo-luminescence studies have revealed that 1 can efficiently distinguish Fe3+ from Fe2+ and other metal ions. On the other hand, 1 also can exhibit a highly sensitive, excellently selective and real-time detection of benzaldehyde and pH through photo-luminescence quenching process. As for 1, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) theory has been applied to calculate these spectroscopic data, the result agree with the experimental results for detection of benzaldehyde. Photo-luminescent recyclability results indicated 1 can be reused at least five times in the detection process. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of a multi-responsive regenerable luminescent sensor for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of Fe3+ over Fe2+, benzaldehyde and pH values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coordination polymer
Ultrasound
Fluorescence
pH sensor
Fe3+
Benzaldehyde
Regenerable material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Density functional theory (DFT) and time dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) computational details
Preparation of {[Zn(L2)(NO3)2]}n (coordination polymer 1) via hydrothermal method
Synthesis of {[Zn(L2)(NO3)2]}n (coordination polymer 1) via sonochemical process
Morphology variation in the ultrasonic synthesis process of 1
Photo-luminescent sensing for pH value by 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fundamental coordination unit of {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1). (b) The porous 2D network of the {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1). (c) Three-dimensional porous hydrogen bond structure of {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1).
Fluorescence emission spectra of free ligand L and coordination polymer 1 in the aqueous (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) when excited at 275 nm.
SEM images of nanostructured CP 1 (a) without ultrasonically synthesized and (b) in the sonochemical time of 60 min at a power of 70 W.
SEM images of nanostructured CP 1 with different sonochemical power (a) 100 W, (b) 70 W.
SEM images of nanostructured CP 1 with different sonochemical time (a) 40 min; (b) 60 min and (c) 80 min.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CP 1 (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) in incremental addition of different solvent molecules solution (50 μL, 0.1 mol/L, ethanol as solvent) when excited at 275 nm. (b) Fluorescence intensities of CP 1 (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by addition of different solvent molecules solution (50 μL, 0.1 mol/L, ethanol as solvent) when excited at 275 nm.

Fluorescence emission spectra, (a) linear relation and (b) intensities of coordination polymer (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by adding different concentrations of benzaldehyde when excited at 275 nm (ethanol as solvent).
Fluorescence intensities of coordination polymer 1 (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by adding of different solvent molecules solution (50 μL, 0.1 mol/L, ethanol as solvent) when excited at 275 nm.
UV–vis spectra of benzaldehyde and coordination polymer 1.

(a) The structure of coordination polymer 1 in ethanol with selected bond lengths (b) calculated UV/vis absorption, and (c) molecular orbital energy diagrams and isodensity surface plots of excited orbitals of coordination polymer 1.
Fluorescence intensities of coordination polymer 1 (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by adding of different solvent molecules solution (150 μL, 0.01 mol/L, ethanol as aqueous) when excited at 275 nm.

Fluorescence emission spectra, (a) linear relation and (b) intensities of coordination polymer (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by adding different concentrations of FeCl3 when excited at 275 nm (ethanol as solvent).
UV–vis spectra of Fe3+ and coordination polymer 1.
Fluorescence emission spectra, (a) linear relation (b) and intensities of coordination polymer (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) by adding different pH buffer when excited at 275 nm (ethanol as solvent).

(a) Cyclic response of luminescence intensities at 310 nm of coordination polymer 1 for detecting benzaldehyde. (b) Cyclic response of luminescence intensities at 310 nm of coordination polymer 1 for detecting Fe3+. (c) Cyclic response of luminescence intensities at 310 nm of coordination polymer 1 for detecting pH value.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical synthesis of a multi-responsive regenerable water-stable zinc(II) fluorescent probe for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of benzaldehyde, ferric ion and PH

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry (Tianjin Normal University)Ministry of EducationTianjin Key Laboratory of Structure and Performance for Functional MoleculeCollege of ChemistryTianjin Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel water-stable coordination polymer with {44} network topology {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1) (L = 4,4′-Bis(triazol-1-ylmethyl)biphenyl) has been synthesized through the hydrothermal and sonochemical approaches. 1 has been characterized by single crystal X-ray diffraction, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectrum and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). PXRD patterns of the as-synthesized samples 1 have confirmed the purity of the bulky samples. In the sonochemical preparation approaches, different ultrasound irradiation power and ultrasound time were also used in order to investigate the impact factor for morphology and size of nano-structured 1. Photo-luminescence studies have revealed that 1 can efficiently distinguish Fe3+ from Fe2+ and other metal ions. On the other hand, 1 also can exhibit a highly sensitive, excellently selective and real-time detection of benzaldehyde and pH through photo-luminescence quenching process. As for 1, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) theory has been applied to calculate these spectroscopic data, the result agree with the experimental results for detection of benzaldehyde. Photo-luminescent recyclability results indicated 1 can be reused at least five times in the detection process. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of a multi-responsive regenerable luminescent sensor for highly selective, sensitive and real-time sensing of Fe3+ over Fe2+, benzaldehyde and pH values.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, more and more attentions have been paid to the luminescent coordination polymers (LCPs) for recognition molecules because of their unique factors, such as their tunable fluorescence intensity, high sensitivity, diverse motifs, novel topologies, simply preparation and high porosity [1–3]. Fluorescence technology is famous for its quick response, high sensitivity, simple operation and low cost [4–7]. It opens new perspectives for employing CPs to detect positive ions, negative ions, organic molecules, biomolecules even the genetic and metabolic activities of a living organism. These applications have significant meaning for qualitative and quantitative analysis and controlling chemicals, furthermore understanding the process of life activities in gene and cell [8].

The ultrasound method is famous for rapid, easy, inexpensive, gram-scaled environmentally friendly and has been widely used in synthesizing CPs [9,10]. Through powerful ultrasound radiation (20 kHz–10 MHz), chemical reaction can be completed fast. In cavitation, ultrasound induces chemical or physical changes, formation, growth, and instantaneously implosive collapse of bubbles in a liquid can be observed [9]. Ultrasound radiation can generate local hot spots with temperatures up to 5000 K, nearly 1000 bar pressures in a few microseconds. These extreme conditions can drive chemical reactions and promote the formation of nano-structures. For the coordination polymer, it mostly via an increase of crystallization nuclei [9]. More crystallization nuclei combined with various solutions also facilitate the formation of the crystal. Therefore, the ultrasound method provided a high yield and rapid method to synthesize these functional coordination polymers [10].

Generally, benzaldehyde is a crucial chemical raw material for industry, since it can be used to the reaction of nucleophilic addition, aldol condensation and conicharo disproportionation [11]. Moreover, it can also be used as medical intermediates to synthesize ephedrine and chloramphenicol [12]. Iron, it is the most indispensable element in our body, shortage of which will cause the iron-deficiency anemia, memory deterioration, child dysplasia and decreased immunity [13]. Unfortunately, surplus usage of iron may result emesis, diarrhea, intestine damage and accelerated human aging [14]. To the life entity, pH is a key element to sustain the internal environment homeostasis [15]. Reversely, abnormal pH such as intracellular acidification (pH 4.5–6.0) often causes many important pathological processes, such as inflammation, tumorgenesis, and cystic fibrosis [15]. Moreover, it also causes the change of activity of the enzyme, which determines the activity of the enzyme such as respiratory action, photosynthesis and tricarboxylic acid cycle [16]. Hence, it has a practical meaning to design a sensitive sensor to detect benzaldehyde, iron and pH values and ensure favorable process in the chemical reaction and protect life entities’ internal environment homeostasis.

1,2,4-Triazole and its derivatives are prominent for their potential bridging modes and intriguing functional properties. Thus they have gained more interest as ligands to bridge different metal ions to form functional coordination polymers. Among these 1,2,4-Triazole derivatives, the rigid multi-dentate ligand (4,4′-Bis (triazol-1-ylmethyl) biphenyl (L)) was adopted due to the following three aspects. Firstly, L contains four potential triazole N-donor coordination sites, which can construct diverse coordination polymers with diverse structural motifs and unique applications; Secondly, nitrogen atoms from L’s rings can offer additional supramolecular interactions to consolidate the framework through hydrogen bond interactions; Thirdly, with rich π electrons, the combination of bi-phenyl rings and triazole rings not only can provide stable and rigid skeletons, but also possess wide conjugation structure. All of these factors can facilitate to generate strong fluorescence intensity, therefore, L can be expected as the ideal building block for the construction of fluorescence coordination polymer.

In this work, a novel water-stable coordination polymer {[Zn(L)2(NO3)2]}n (1) (L = 4,4′-Bis(triazol-1-ylmethyl)biphenyl) (Scheme 1 ) with {44} network topology has been synthesized through the hydrothermal and sonochemical approaches. Sonochemical approach provided a rapid and convenient method for synthesizing the coordination polymer 1. In comparison with other coordination polymers, 1 has some advantages because 1 is water-stable and has high Ksv values in effectively distinguishing Fe3+ over Fe2+ and other metal ions. On the other hand, 1 also can exhibit a highly sensitive, excellently selective and real-time detection of benzaldehyde and pH. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculation results also explain the reason why 1 has high photo-luminescence quenching effect toward benzaldehyde. Photo-luminescent recyclability experiments also reveal that 1 has excellent cyclicality in the detection process. Therefore 1 can be applied as an ideal photo-luminescent sensor towards Benzaldehyde, Ferric ion and PH [2,3,17–19].

The L Ligand was prepared according to reported methods [20]. All reagents were purchased commercially and used without further purification. Deionized water was used as solvent in this work. C, H, and N microanalyses were carried out with a Perkin-Elmer 240 elemental analyzer. Poweder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was determined on a D/Max-2500 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation. FT-IR spectra (4000–500 cm−1) were recorded using a NICOLET 6700 FT-IR spectroscope with KBr pellets (NICOLET, USA). ultrasonic preparation was carried by a SB-100DT Ultrasonic bath (XJ Biotechnology Instrument, ZheJiang, china). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) adsorption spectra were collected on a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Photo-luminescent sensing measurements were performed on an Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) equipped with a plotter unit and a quartz cell (1 cm × 1 cm) in the phosphorescence mode. The morphology and size of 1 prepared by hydrothermal and sonochemical method were characterized by Nova-Nano 230 scanning electron microscope (SEM; FEI instrument, USA) with gold coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density functional theory (DFT) and time dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) computational details
ce:section.ce:para: DFT and TDDFT calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 program [21] and the molecular orbitals were analyzed using Gaussview software. CAM-B3LYP functional with LANL2DZ basis set were used for all atoms, because this method has an excellent performance for studying UV Spectroscope of Zn-containing complexes [22]. All compounds were full optimized with the SMD solvation model and the ethanol solvent in the experiments was used for solvent effects. Once convergence is reached, the harmonic vibration frequencies were performed at this point to confirm the geometry obtained to be true minima. The first 20 excited states of coordination polymer were calculated in all TDDFT calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of {[Zn(L2)(NO3)2]}n (coordination polymer 1) via hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of cadmium iodide (59.5 mg, 0.2 mmol) and L (31.6 mg, 0.1 mmol) was added into deionized water (5 mL). The mixed solutions were transferred into a Teflon vessel and then put into a steelautoclave. Subsequently, it was heated at 140 °C for 72 h and then cooled to room temperature over the period of 24 h. The resulting colorless block-shaped crystals were washed several times by water. The yield of 1 was 54% based on H2L. Elemental analysis results (wt%) for C36H32N14O6Zn: C 52.48, H 3.77, N 23.52; calcd: C 52.59, H 3.92, N 23.85. FT-IR data (cm−1): 3100 (w), 1511 (m), 1438 (m), 1317 (m), 1268 (s), 1128(m), 1013 (s), 862 (w), 753 (s), 678(s), 640(w).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of {[Zn(L2)(NO3)2]}n (coordination polymer 1) via sonochemical process
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of cadmium iodide (59.5 mg, 0.2 mmol) and L (31.6 mg, 0.1 mmol) was added into deionized water (5 mL). The mixed solutions were transferred in the ultrasonic bath. Then, the ultrasonic bath was performed for 60 min, at a power of 100 W. The resulting precipitates were isolated by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and dried in air. The yield of 1: 65% based on H2L. Elemental analysis results (wt%) for C36H32N14O6Zn: C 52.50, H 3.87, N 23.42; calcd: C 52.59, H 3.92, N 23.85. FT-IR data (cm−1): 3100 (w), 1511 (m), 1426 (m), 1345 (m), 1316 (m), 1270 (s), 1128 (m), 1014 (m), 861 (w), 836 (w), 802 (w), 754(m), 678(m), 641(w).

Diffraction intensities of 1 were collected on a Bruker SMART 1000 CCD diffractometer with graphite-monochromated Mo-Kα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å) using the ω-φ scan technique. Lorentz polarization and absorption corrections were applied. The structure was solved by direct methods and refined with the full-matrix least-squares technique using the SHELXS-97 and SHELXL-97 programs. Anisotropic thermal parameters were assigned to all non-hydrogen atoms. The organic hydrogen atoms were generated geometrically; the hydrogen atoms of the water molecules were located from different maps and refined with isotropic temperature factors. Analytical expressions of neutral-atom scattering factors were employed, and anomalous dispersion corrections were incorporated [23]. The crystallographic data and details of refinements for coordination polymer 1 were summarized in Table S1, the selected bond lengths and angles were listed in Table S2, and the selected hydrogen bonds lengths [Å] and angles [°] calculated with the program PLATON were listed in Table S3. CCDC-1438185 contained the supplementary crystallographic data for this work. These data can be obtained free of charge from The Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre via www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk/data_request/cif.

Single crystal X-ray analysis revealed that 1 crystallized in the triclinic space group P-1 (Table S1). The asymmetric unit of 1 contained one zinc center (Zn1), two L ligands and two terminal coordination water molecules and two free nitrate anions. Each Zn (II) ion, lying in distorted octahedral coordination geometry, was six-coordinated by four nitrogen ions through the chelating mode and two oxygen atoms through the end-base coordination mode (Fig. 1 a). The Zn-O bond length was 2.1728 (15) Å, while Zn-N bond lengths varied from 2.1115(17) Å to 2.1465(17) Å. The bond angles of N-Zn-N ranged from 89.30°(7) to 180°, the bond angles of N-Zn-O ranged from 85.51°(6) to 94.49°(6), and the bond angle of O-Zn-O was 180° (Table S2). Both bond length and angles can be compared with previous literature [24]. It was noteworthy that each Zn(II) ion was linked four L ligands, and each L was linked two neighboring Zn (II) ions, thus ultimately generating the two-dimensional (2D) network (Fig. 1b). Through reducing the multidimensional structure to a simple node-linker reference net, the structure of coordination polymer 1 can be clarified, which was known as the topological analysis approach [25]. Therefore, in this 2D network, the point (Schläfli) symbol for the net was 44. In addition, these coordinated oxygen and nitrogen atoms from ligand were involved C-H···O and C-H···N hydrogen bonds (C-H···O angles were between 154° and 164° while C-H···N angles were between 138° and 164°). With the hydrogen bond interaction, three-dimensional supramolecular architecture can be extended (Fig. 1c). Such weak interactions also played a crucial role in stabilizing the whole network.

PXRD patterns of 1 under hydrothermal conditions and sonochemical approaches were also recorded at room temperature to determine the phase purity. As shown in Fig. S1, the experimental patterns were in good agreement with the simulated ones, implying phase the as-synthesized samples of 1 were pure under the hydrothermal reaction and sonochemical approaches. It was also noted that different sonochemical time and ultrasound irradiation power did not change the structure of these as-synthesized 1 (Fig. S2). The slight differences in reflection intensities between the simulated and the experimental patterns were due to the variation in the crystal orientation of the powder sample.

FT-IR spectra of 1 were recorded to display characteristic absorption bands for triazole, and phenyl moieties of L (Fig. S3). The peaks located at ca. 3100 cm−1 and 1100–1300 cm−1 can be attributed to ν (C-H) and ν (C-N) or ν (N-N) of triazole moieties. The triazole out of plane ring absorption was at 630 cm−1. The benzene ring exhibited medium peaks around 1500 cm−1 [26]. The medium narrow peaks ca. 860 cm−1, indicated the phenyl groups in the para-orientation mode.

Based on rich π-electron in aromatic organic ligands, inorganic-organic hybrid coordinated polymers have been determined for promising photo-luminescent materials [27]. Because L has the rich π-electron and wide conjugated structure, 1 had strong luminescence intensity. The luminescence properties of free ligand L and 1 in aqueous solutions were investigated at room temperature. The free ligand L and 1 in the aqueous solution (0.067 g/L, 3 mL) displayed emissions band begin at 295 nm (excited at 285 nm) (Fig. 2 ). The chromospheres were the aromatic bi-phenyl and triazole rings, and the observed emissions were due to π–π* transition. In comparison with ligand L, 1 had the same emission band at 310 nm, which should be ascribed to ligand-to-ligand charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology variation in the ultrasonic synthesis process of 1

ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the fluorescent quenching properties of benzaldehyde derivatives (p-nitrobenzaldehyde, methyl-p-oxybenzaldehyde, 4-hydroxy benzaldehyde), the fine grinding samples of 1 (2 mg) were immersed in ethanol solvent (3 mL) and ultrasonicated 15 min to form stable suspensions for luminescent experiment. Then, three aromatic aldehydes (0.1 M, 50 μL) were added in suspensions. Fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 8 ) showed that the three aromatic aldehydes can also efficiently quench the fluorescence and the photo-luminescence intensities were largely dependent on the substituent groups. Comparing with other aromatic aldehydes, p-nitrobenzaldehyde with two strong electron withdrawing groups, namely nitro and aldehyde groups, had a higher quenching efficiency of approximately 99%.

It is noted that PXRD patterns did not change during the photo-luminescent sensing process for benzaldehyde values by 1 (Fig. S2). The mechanism for coordination polymer’s quenching effect to benzaldehyde was also discussed, the UV-absorption spectra of benzaldehyde and 1 in ethanol solution were monitored during 190–450 nm (Fig. 9 ). The spectrum of 1 directly overlapped with benzaldehyde’s, which indicated the competition of absorption of the light source energy between benzaldehyde and organic ligands. The energy absorbed by the ligands was transferred to benzaldehyde molecules, resulting in a decrease in the luminescence intensity [12]. In addition, the quenching efficiency was consistent with electron-withdrawing abilities of the substituent groups. Electron-deficient benzaldehyde can gain an electron from an excited ligand, which was illustrated by molecular orbital theory. Therefore, the excited state electrons can transfer from the coordination network of 1 (electron donor) to benzaldehyde (electron acceptor). Generally, energy and election transfer may play an intrinsic role in the process of luminescence quenching for benzaldehyde towards 1.

DFT and TDDFT were stimulated to reveal the mechanism, coordination polymer 1 in ethanol formed a six-coordinated (twisted octahedral) structure. The zinc ion was surrounded by two nitrate anion as axial ligand and four L ligands as equatorial bidentate ligand. The chelating structure of the complexes reduced the freedom of the ligand vibration, reducing the energy dissipation and improved luminous efficiency. A broad band (238–259 nm) was ascribed to the ligand-to-ligand charge transfer absorption according to DFT calculations (Fig. 10 ). In this process, the anti-bond orbitals of nitrate anion were slightly involved. However, the d orbitals of zinc ions were completely independent with the electron excitation (Section I and Section II in supporting information). In the coordination polymer 1, the electron transition from HOMO to LUMO + 2, and HOMO-1 to LUMO + 3 were participated in the strongest ultraviolet absorption. The experimental UV-absorption spectrum was good well with calculated simulated spectrum, confirming the correctness of this calculation method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-luminescent sensing for pH value by 1
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical resistance of coordination polymer 1 was examined by soaking in buffer solutions which are pH of 2–8 buffer solutions for 24 h. The experimental PXRD patterns of samples were strongly agreement with as-simulated, inferring the integrity of the framework under these conditions. Fluorescence experiments of pH were performed in different citric acid-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solutions (2 mL) with a probe concentration of 0.067 g/L (2 mL). When excited at 275 nm, the emission intensity at 310 nm gradually decreased as the decreasing the pH from 8 to 2 (Fig. 14 ). The quenching effect coefficient of coordination polymer 1 was calculated according equation 1. It had excellent linear relation between intensity and concentration (R2 = 0.9776), the value of Ksv was 1.5 × 105 for CP 1 at low concentration which indicated high sensitive.

The mechanism for pH sensing by coordination polymer 1 was investigated, the mechanism for pH sensing was investigated, which may be the energy transfer from ligand to metal centers. It is noted that PXRD patterns does not change during the photo-luminescent sensing process for pH values by 1 (Fig. S2). Under ionization effect and hydrolytic action, H+ and OH− existed in different pH value citric acid-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solutions. The protons were captured by N atoms, in which the charge transfer between the ligand and the metal ions was likely to be tuned by the hydrogen bonding between the ligands and H+ thus leading to an increase in charge-transfer-based emission intensity and a decrease in the ligand-based emission intensity [31].

The reusability of a sensor was very vital for practical application [32]. Hence, recyclability experiments on the detection of benzaldehyde (0.1 M, 4 mL), Fe3+ (0.01 M, 4 mL) and pH in two different buffer solution (4 mL) were carried out. Used samples were recovered by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and aqueous solution, respectively, to remove their solvents and added analytes. The above operation was repeated five times. As shown in Fig. 15 , photo-luminescent intensity of 1 could be reused at least five times. The PXRD patterns of these samples after five recycle experiments were still in excellent agreement with those of the simulated patterns, implying the as-synthesized samples were pure and maintained its origin structure after the recycle experiments [33] (Fig. S2).

In this work, a unique water-stable coordination polymer {[Zn(L2)(NO3)2]}n (1) with 2D network has been designed through the powerful hydrothermal and ultrasonic method. Structural analysis revealed that 1 was a two-dimensional (2D) coordination polymer with {44} network topology. Photo-luminescence studies have revealed that 1 can efficiently distinguish Fe3+ from Fe2+ and other metal ions through fluorescent quenching effect. In addition, 1 can exhibit a highly sensitive, excellently selective and real-time detection of benzaldehyde and pH through photo-luminescence quenching process. To measure their reasonable dosage, fluorescence detection was preferred to be adopted. As for 1, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) simulated result well agreed with the experimental results for detection of benzaldehyde. Photo-luminescent recyclability results indicated 1 could be reused at least five times in the detection process. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of a multi-responsive regenerable coordination polymer based luminescent sensor for Fe3+, benzaldehyde and pH values with good linear relation and high Ksv values. This work proved the coordination framework-based photo-luminescent sensors were sensitive to environmental relevant species, which may be useful under realistic conditions in the future.

This work was financially supported by the Open Project of Key Lab Adv Energy Mat Chem (Nankai Univ), (111 project, B12015), Tianjin Educational Committee (20120508), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (Grant no. 14JCYBJC22400, 12JCZDJC21700, 15JCYBJC48200 and 14JCQNJC05900), Young Scientist Fund (Grant no. 21301128), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375095, 20975054), Innovation Fund of Tianjin Normal University (52XC1603) and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD12-5038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.02.048.
